content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. A beautiful, profound movie that might even make me shed a tear

**Score:** 90
**Body:** I found myself complaining sitting at my work desk at home and was suddenly hit by a yearning to watch something powerful tonight. What have you got?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Cinema Paradiso. It won the Oscar for Best Foreign Film in 1989. It is a film for people you love Film. You will shed a tear.
2. Perfect Days (2023)
3. Ameliè
4. Big Fish hits all the marks
5. The Diving Bell and the Butterfly 2007
6. Arrival (2016), Interstellar (2014), Children of Men (2006), Gattaca (1997), Magnolia (1999)
7. What Dreams May Come

The Fisher King
8. The Sweet Hereafter (1997), my most profound cinematic experience ever.
9. A Single Man (2009) will stop your heart.
10. Ordinary People (1980)

## 2. Movies breaking the fourth wall in the best way possible.

**Score:** 66
**Body:** Topic. What are some of the movies which directly speak to the audience. Not a movie but House of card has done this in a good way during its earlier seasons. Wolf of Wall Street, The Big Short are some of other good examples. Looking for more such movies. NO SPOILERS. only recommend movies names . All language, country and year movies welcome.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Wayne’s World
2. Ferris Beuler’s Day Off

Spaceballs

Annie Hall
  - You’re still here? It’s over. Go home.
3. Jay and Silent Bob Strike Back.
4. Airplane - “what a pisser”
5. Blazing Saddles (1974)

Goodfellas (1990)
  - I think Blazing Saddles is my favourite, where at the end they *literally* break the fourth wall.
6. Funny Games (1997).
7. Bill &Ted’s Excellent Adventure

“They do get better”
8. Mel Brooks’ producing career!
Michael York talking about Time Travel, and how to deal with it!

## 3. Long ass Movies

**Score:** 26
**Body:** Some long movies (let's say more than 2hr 30mins). 

I know of The Human Condition Trilogy and working on sourcing it.  

I like most movies so don't worry about it and I'll update this post based on things that are suggested that I've seen. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Lawrence of Arabia (3h 36m)
Seven Samurai (3h 27m)
The Godfather Part II (3h 22m)
Ben-Hur (3h 32m) 
Once Upon a Time in America (3h 49m)
2. Frankenstein (2025), just released, is 2.5hrs.
3. Satantango by Bela Tarr is 7hr 30m

Also one of my faves in a similar vein is Tarkovsly's Stalker
4. I remember when the Titanic came out and it was a double VHS and 14-year-old me was like "what in the holy hell?" Now there are movies even longer than Titanic. Crazy.
5. The Brutalist
6. Barry Lyndon (1975). 3h 5m
7. Several of Scorsese’s works fit into this category: The Departed (2h 31 m), Killers of the Flower Moon (3h 26m), Silence (2h 41m), Casino (2h 58 m), The Irishman (3h 29m), The Last Temptation of Christ (2h 43m), I could go on
  - why  not- he did
8. Until the End of the World (Wim Wenders) - 4hrs 47min
9. Cloud Atlas (2012)

Gulliver's Travels (1996)

The Lord of the Rings Trilogy (2001/2002/2003)

## 4. Need a horror movie to watch with friends.

**Score:** 20
**Body:** I am looking for a new horror movie with good plot. I liked insideous as the plot was very good, didn't like sinister as the story lead to nothing, conjuring was average for me as the doll genre is not too interesting for me.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Talk to Me and It Follows were my favorite group watches!
2. Cabin in the wood 2011 is what u watch with friends
3. Weapons, thoroughly enjoyed it and you will never in a million years guess how it ends 💙
4. r/horror this is the best sub to ask
5. Barbarian
6. I watched Bodies Bodies Bodies with my friends and we had the time of our lives.
7. Hereditary
8. Get Out
9. [Deadstream](https://www.rottentomatoes.com/m/deadstream) (2022)

It’s great watch with friends, and has a strong [Evil Dead 2](https://www.rottentomatoes.com/m/evil_dead_ii) vibe.
10. The Evil Dead trilogy is so fucking fun with friends

## 5. Movies with smart villains and the hero wins by dragging the villain down to their stupidity

**Score:** 17
**Body:** “The best swordsman does not fear the second best. He fears the worst since there’s no telling what that idiot is going to do.” Something kinda like this quote. 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I feel like The Emperor’s New Groove must fall into this category.
2. I feel like Guardians of the Galaxy is a decent fit here
  - What, you don't really think a villain would fall for the 'dance off' maneuver?
3. **Drunken Master**, 1978


**The Legend of Drunken Master**, 1994
4. Ernest Goes to Camp 

Ernest Scared Stupid
5. Happy Gilmore
  - Add Billy Maddison in there too
6. "Big Trouble in Little China" (1986)
7. Not a movie but for tv shows SpongeBob and Patrick immediately come to mind
8. Cabin in the woods ? Marty specifically. The guy smoking so much pot that increases his paranoia ( hearing voices etc..) to the point where he figures out early on that something isn't right and that fact he's so baked it makes him immune to the subliminal and chemical mind control against him.

  
EDIT:  He managed to sneak a secret stash of weed in that wasn't tampered with by the company to suit their needs

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Gen Z Wants More Animation and Less Sex Onscreen, According to Teens & Screens Survey

**Score:** 1235
**Body:** What are your thoughts on this emerging trend among newer cinemagoers and how it might affect filmmaking in the future?

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I feel like there is less sex on screen (compared to the 70’s, 80’s, 90’s & 00’s). 


Even fewer nude scenes.
  - The podcast “you must remember this” did a series on erotic 80s cinema and how in contrast to the great and erotic films that were common back then, today’s films are largely sexless. 

A big factor is that in the 70s and 80s it was harder to see nudity in general. If it unexpectedly happened in a movie it felt exciting and was less sleazy than watching an outright porno or buying magazines. Nowadays, everyone has a phone in their pocket that they could instantly find nudity if they wanted to.

I do think there has been an over sanitisation of media in this regard and some audiences become too puritanical or conservative about things like nudity, when it’s just a fact of life
2. How are they doing these kinda survey, is my question. If I was a teenager, obviously I wouldn't go on record saying "YEAH!! me likey Sweeney boobies!".
  - That's always the important question when it comes to these because it's always funny how they serve to perpetuate an agenda that has already been pushed, but has come to meet some resistance recently.
3. What about more animated sex?
4. You know, twenty something years ago, I remember all the crazy conspiracy theorists going on about how the "people who rule us", wanted to reduce the world population and do it with chemicals, make us not want kids, make us disinterested in sex etc etc etc.

I know this is random to mention in a movie sub, but titles like this just make me think, "shit, were those lunatics onto something?" 😂
5. Is there even any sex left in shows?

Kinda weird how little there is tbh its as tho they forget it can be used in a good way to drive character development 

Instead its treated like sex is scary and never to be seen or utilised in shows and movies

Also yea we need more western animation arcane and invincible prove that
6. What sex on screen?! The late millenials and gen Z have already pretty much nuked it from all entertainment. Good lord.
  - Anora was full of sex scenes and won Best Picture.  Poor Things from the year before was full of sex scenes as well.
    - I also think "Honey Don't" was made just to have Softcore porn scenes with Margaret Qualley and Aubrey Plaza

## 2. Waiting - Mitch's rant at the end of the movie was absolutely epic.

**Score:** 1212
**Body:** * [Mitch](https://www.imdb.com/name/nm0197855/?ref_=ttch_qu): \[*after being interrupted yet again, Mitch has had enough*\] Hey turn down the music for minute... Hey would you turn down the music?
* **Kid at Stereo**: Dude, chill.
* [Mitch](https://www.imdb.com/name/nm0197855/?ref_=ttch_qu): Would you turn down the fucking music for a minute! Jesus!
* \[*Mitch turns the power off on the stereo and turns to everyone*\]
* [Mitch](https://www.imdb.com/name/nm0197855/?ref_=ttch_qu): This is fucking bullshit! I have been here all goddamned day and you haven't let me say one thing! None of you!
* [Monty](https://www.imdb.com/name/nm0005351/?ref_=ttch_qu): Well, damn, Mitch, I...
* [Mitch](https://www.imdb.com/name/nm0197855/?ref_=ttch_qu): Oh, no, asshole! You shut the fuck up now. It's my turn to talk! You're all fucked in the head! All of you! I mean you.
* \[*points at Naomi*\]
* [Mitch](https://www.imdb.com/name/nm0197855/?ref_=ttch_qu): Change your fuckin' tampon and have another drink you crazy, fuckin' bitch!
* \[*Points at Dean*\]
* [Mitch](https://www.imdb.com/name/nm0197855/?ref_=ttch_qu): And you! "Waaahh, I don't know what to be when I grow up!" Join the fucking army or something! Goddamn.
* \[*Points at Calvin*\]
* [Mitch](https://www.imdb.com/name/nm0197855/?ref_=ttch_qu): Oh, and you! You know what? You're too easy. And you.
* \[*Points to Monty*\]
* [Mitch](https://www.imdb.com/name/nm0197855/?ref_=ttch_qu): FUCK YOU MONTY! Always gotta be right, with your little quips! We get it, man. You're fuckin' edgy and cool. Yeah! You're the coolest fuckin' guy at Shenaniganz! WHOOO! That's like being the smartest kid with Down syndrome! Oh and, oh, yeah. Why aren't you in jail? I mean what
* \[*looks at Natasha*\]
* [Mitch](https://www.imdb.com/name/nm0197855/?ref_=ttch_qu): are you like 13, 14?
* [Monty](https://www.imdb.com/name/nm0005351/?ref_=ttch_qu): She's almost 18.
* [Mitch](https://www.imdb.com/name/nm0197855/?ref_=ttch_qu): You know what? Fuck this! You all suck. I quit.
* \[*Goes to leave and gets to the door and turns around*\]
* [Mitch](https://www.imdb.com/name/nm0197855/?ref_=ttch_qu): Oh, and yeah. There is one more thing.
* \[*Points at Floyd*\]
* [Mitch](https://www.imdb.com/name/nm0197855/?ref_=ttch_qu): You... You are the biggest piece of shit in this entire restaurant.
* \[*Floyd looks around to see if Mitch is actually talking to him*\]
* [Mitch](https://www.imdb.com/name/nm0197855/?ref_=ttch_qu): And I hope you burn in hell.
* [Floyd](https://www.imdb.com/name/nm0176981/?ref_=ttch_qu): Me? What the fuck did I do to you, man? Seriously?
* [Raddimus](https://www.imdb.com/name/nm0350079/?ref_=ttch_qu): \[*Mitch pulls down his pants and everyone says "Oh shit!*\] The goat! The goat, you bastard!
* [Mitch](https://www.imdb.com/name/nm0197855/?ref_=ttch_qu): \[*Mitch pulls up his pants and opens the door and walks out*\] Fuckin' faggots.
* [Monty](https://www.imdb.com/name/nm0005351/?ref_=ttch_qu): That was the shit!
* \[*he runs out after Mitch*\]
* [Monty](https://www.imdb.com/name/nm0005351/?ref_=ttch_qu): Mitch! Mitch! Stop please. Look, look. Stop, stop. Okay I am sorry, and I hearby swear my undying allegiance to you. You are the fucking man.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This was such a great scene, was not allowed to talk the entire movie due to constant interruptions and then lets loose and destroys everyone.
  - "You.  You are the biggest piece of shit in this entire restaurant.  I hope you burn in hell."
    - “What the fuck did *I* do, man?!”
      - 🐐
      - The way everyone turns to look at Dane Cook, and then he looks around behind him
  - Luiz Guzman drops trow and lays in wait to give the goat to someone then he realizes he’s not alone….”Hey Mitch what’s up man!”
2. He's become a really good director, too. Game Night and DnD: Honor Among Thieves were great efforts
  - I actually just watched “Freaks and Geeks” for the first time last month. 

I was a little surprised when I went back and checked out some Where Are They Now articles. 

He also did the sequel to the National Lampoon’s Vacation series which is pretty awesome.
3. "The goat! The goat, you bastard!"
4. The line about down syndrome was and is still brutal

## 3. The Remake or The Original?

**Score:** 804

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Dredd isn't a remake. They're both separate adaptations of the character Judge Dredd from 2000 A.D.
  - Yeah, they are totally different adaptations and totally different stories.

Also it's Dredd and it's not close.
    - Karl Urban was perfect as Dredd.  Couldn’t have cast it better.
      - Aside from the outstanding acting from Urban, dude made it so much better by *not* taking off the helmet. Stallone was way too arrogant to let his face stay covered the whole move, and in turn, ruined the original movie. Imo.
    - Loved the Staline movie growing up, then I found the comics….. the. Dredd came out and I’ve been dying for a sequel or an HBO type series since
2. Judge Dredd is a nostalgic guilty pleasure with little to no redeeming qualities beyond a good laugh.

Dredd is a genuinely fantastic movie that has one small downside - it was made during the "EVERYTHING MUST HAVE BULLET TIME 3D STUFF" time period. However, it doesn't date Dredd as much as other movies of that time because of how it's used in the story.
  - Yeah but the bullet time was a plot point. A dumb one sure, but it worked
3. The only things I don’t like about Stallone’s Judge Dredd are that he takes his mask off and the comic relief character played by Rob Schneider. Everything else about it is a fun movie.

The only thing I don’t like about Dredd is that there hasn’t been another one yet. Urban isn’t too old. The Boys is almost done. There’s no reason we can’t get another $30-$45 million Dredd movie for PPV, then digital purchase then standard streaming, you’d no doubt make that money back as a streaming house funding it. Then there’s the physical copies people would want to buy. With the great word of mouth it could have online you’d save a ton in marketing and advertising.

I’m still out here hoping away
4. The second one was lit
5. Dredd beats Judge Dredd by a country mile

## 4. Purely acting wise, ignoring filmography and consistency, who'd you say is the best among the lot?

**Score:** 488

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Can I just say this is an excellent lineup?  You could make an argument for any of them. Usually these things contain at least one actor that no one votes for but this list is an actual competition.  Well done!
2. Lately Pattinson delivers the goods man.
  - Definitely in the last few years. Maybe just because he's more active, but I've enjoyed his recent performances a lot. Seems a very wide ranged actor.
3. Tom hardy prob unpopular opinion but I think he really is a different person when he takes on a role.
4. Cant believe there's only a few comments abt cillian. 

Cillian, Jake and Tom for me
5. Mad to think Nolan had 3 of these in the same movie with Inception
6. Pitt wasn’t given the roles Leo got. Pitt for me
  - Pitt is really consistent and versatile. He's pretty much always good.
7. Jake G
  - “Each year we’re presented with a film that asks the question - is Jake Gyllenhaal OK?” - Amanda Dobbins. 
I can picture Jake G playing almost any of the roles these other actors have played. How many of the above actors could have been the music man in John Mulaney’s Sack Lunch Bunch?

## 5. Which one is most dangerous

**Score:** 211
**Body:** Which one is the definition of absolute evil.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Lecter respects good manners and artistic talent. He might even invite you to dinner. 

Anton is a dude who can find insult in saying good afternoon.
  - Excuse me

If Hannibal invites you to dinner, you may well be the main course.  

That’s how he gets all his victims. He’s a respectable professional erudite highly social man. Who can cook a hell of a dish.

He’s so much more insidious than Anton Chigurh and his “something is very wrong with me” haircut.  
    - Yeah it's amazing how much PR Lector has manipulated people into doing for him simply by being classy. Remember that this is the guy that will bite a nurse's face off just because. He's a violent psychopath but he's also extremely manipulative and will make you want to say nice things about him. I think he's the more dangerous one. Chigurh just has that remorseless unstoppable force energy.
      - I'm sure his guards in Memphis thought he was just a creepy pansy up until he attacked

"Ready when you are, Sgt. Pembry"
2. Lecter could manipulate the fbi and escaped from prison and traveled other country.
  - But if you treat him with kindness and do not insult his sensibilities you will be fine.
    - Unless you attempt to take the United States census
3. Anton killed for fun. Hannibal killed when he was peckish. Tough one 😂
4. I feel like you have to give Hannibal a reason to kill you whereas Anton will find a reason to kill you
5. call it

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Thoughts on Del Toro's Frankenstein

**Score:** 35
**Body:** Question, What are your thoughts on Del Toro’s Frankenstein?

Now that Del Toro’s Frankenstein is finally out on Netflix, I decided to watch the film and I must say, I really do enjoy the film. 

The film is really what I expected out of a Del Toro Film, the set designs, the costumes, and the effects are all top notch. The acting is good too with good performances from Oscar Isaac, Christoph Waltz, Jacob Elordi, Mia Goth, & Charles Dance. On watching the film, I agree with most that Jacob Eloridi stands out as the monster. Just watching the film, you can tell Del Toro really had a passion for this project, and given the history, he really tried and failed to get his version of Frankenstein off the ground, so it is really a joy to see him get the chance to make the film.

I will say, maybe one criticism of the film is that Del Toro is probably too sympathetic with the monster and putting full blame on Victor whereas in the book, they both are to blame and also the fact that one should not play God. But overall, I do like the film and now after watching it, I wonder what Del Toro will do next.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Hit the nail on the head with that final paragraph. He completely dilutes the moral complexity of the book, which I think is an issue with a lot of his movies. Not the best writer for nuance, he seems prefer simple moral binaries.
  - I think you make a great point. But how could any portrayal of a grave robbing mad scientist not paint him as the villain . I think the way Victor imprisons the monster in the movie seemed a little out of character and evil. But let's be real the guy obviously had very little moral character outside of his obsession
    - Yeha, you still walk away from the book thinking that Victor is morally abhorrent, but the beauty of the book is that Shelley still finds room for sympathy despite ferociously criticising Victor’s actions. Yet, in the movie, Del Toro seems hell bent on portraying Victor as one dimensional villain. I think this becomes most obvious with his change to Elizabeth’s death. In a similar fashion to the monster, in the book he literally kills a child, and yet, once we hear his story, we still feel sympathy. It’s this moral murkiness that the movie completely lacks.
    - > But how could any portrayal of a grave robbing mad scientist not paint him as the villain

Most of the anatomical science we know today was developed thanks to dissections that doctors did with cadavers often obtained with unsavoury means, because it was straight up illegal to do dissections at all. Obviously as a practice it ain't going to win any ethical research awards, but you have to see it in the context of the era - the 19th century wasn't the 16th any more but there was still a fair amount of grave-robbing going on and I don't think any of the current ways for cadavers to be legally acquired for scientific research were in place. So to some extent the dichotomy of "if you want medicine to advance, you need to be willing to dirty your hands, for the sake of the living" was far less extreme and crazy that it might sound to us today.
2. Didn't like it at all.

I think the film looks very bad. It has such a digital flat look I found it distracting. The colour correction, the digital sheen everything compounded together makes it look really weird and inorganic, which is a feat considering how many real sets he has. The CGI used is also woeful. It was hard for me to get over this, I could never get into the film.

Thematically it has no nuance. Victor is the monster (we're endlessly hit over the head with it) and the monster is an innocent scared puppy. There's not much to it, no moral ambiguity, nothing to think about.

Oscar Isaac hams it up to almost comedy level, which is at odds with some of the more serious themes that Del Toro tries to explore. Mia Goth is superfluous, I really never got the feeling that there're any emotions between her and the monster or her and Frankenstein. It's vacuous for me. I liked a few scenes here and there but overall I was very disappointed. I also hated how Del Toro always uses a steadicam and moves the camera around.
  - All of this plus it is deadly dull.
  - The way he glides the camera around without any motivation is so strange. I can't imagine he would have composed shots in this way back when he was shooting on film. Something about digital makes certain directors just stop giving a shit, they just point the camera at whatever's happening and say "good enough." (There are a few good shots in the movie, it's not all bad... but a lot of it really is).
    - I’m as much of a film grain whore as anyone, but is this really the case on the movement front? I wasn’t a big fan of Nightmare Alley in terms of the writing, but the cinematography was pretty decent and that was shot digitally. Although you could argue he was being more restrained to imitate an older style. Like is a digital camera *that* much easier to glide around and shoot where it starts ruining compositions and framing? Not every director is Iñárritu who apparently has waged a war against the tripod or keeping the camera static 

Side note, my favorite Del Toro film is The Devil’s Backbone and the cinematography is so damn good and straightforward.
3. Honestly, it was a bummer to me. Overly long, riddled with atrocious CGI and uncanny digital processing. Unnecessary characters and strange diversions from the source material. As another commenter mentioned, I don’t need a movie to be an exact interpretation of the book to enjoy it but to remove any sense of the overall moral ambiguity inherent in the story was a bad choice. Also, why does The Creature need Marvel level super powers. Dumb.
4. I really enjoyed it, but there was something about it I didn't love and it took a bit for me to figure out what it was. 

Visually it's stunning, but for me the world he created didn't fit the characters that were in it, if that makes sense. The characters sometimes felt like they belonged in either a much darker version of the events, or a much lighter one, but seldom the one they were in.

If every film is a puzzle, and the pieces are each aspect of the film, I.e the sound, the cinematography, the effects etc, this film felt like all the pieces individually were gorgeous, but they all didn't quite fit with each other perfectly. Some fit most of the time, but very rarely did they all fit. That's my take, anyway.

## 2. Best John Cassavetes acting performances?

**Score:** 12
**Body:** Hey all - love the man’s films as a director, but also very impressed by his presence in Rosemary’s Baby. He adds so much nuance and zest to that film.

Are there any other roles where Cassavetes really shines for you as an actor? I’m taking recs, and having seen the likes of Husbands, Opening Night and Love Streams, I’m hoping to get some films he didn’t direct in the mix here!

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. You mentioned several of my favorites already. Otherwise, I'd recommend Mikey & Nicky first and foremost. 

The Columbo episode he was in, Etude in Black, is also terrific. 

I'm a big fan of him as an actor too, and I really enjoyed watching Johnny Staccato. I don't know what the availability is now--when I watched it, I had to buy bootleg VHS tapes off eBay. The show didn't ask him to do a lot, and considering his ability, he could have slept through most of it, but there are definitely some cool moments.
  - YES, a Columbo reference. Etude in Black is one of my favorites
2. Mikey and Nicky has a really great performance by him as well as Peter Falk. When I first watched it I thought it must have been directed by Cassevetes but Elaine May does a fantastic job representing male friendship.
3. Guy touching Rosemary’s stomach after the party to feel the baby kick and quickly drawing it back in disgust of what he did was amazing. Also the way he looked away at roman’s house when Rosemary walked in at the end was great. I really need to see more of his work.
4. Well, he's my favorite in a film with a stacked cast- The Dirty Dozen!  Franko is the jerk you end up relating to and rooting for.  Maybe not an original answer, but he's so great in that movie.
  - He also worked with Lee Marvin (and Ronald Reagan) in The Killers, a noir film that's worth a watch.
  - He’s pretty good as the bad guy in The Fury.
    - Damn, I haven’t thought about The Fury in awhile, crazy movie!
5. Semi related but I absolutely love his appearance on Dick Cavett with Peter Falk & Ben Gazzara. Rosemary’s Baby is what got me interested in him as an actor, then I went down the rabbit hole of his directorial work from there. Here’s that talk show appearance: https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=x-kPTmM_zMc&pp=0gcJCR4Bo7VqN5tD
  - I genuinely appreciate you providing the link and I was kind of excited to see it.

The reality is, I could only get a few minutes in before scrubbing to see if it got any better. 

It didn’t. 

It was 3 very drunk buddies deciding to fuck with the host. There’s a version of that that I’m sure is interesting, but this was just awkward. Watching Cavett drown while the 3 of them drunkenly amused themselves was not anyone’s best work 

## 3. Shoot the Piano Player - The Neoist of Neo-Noir

**Score:** 9
**Body:** I’m doing a deep dive into Trauffaunt and have recently revisited Shoot The Piano Player — and wow, what a noir film. I have to say that this one of the freshest noir films that I‘ve ever watched, simply because it’s trying so hard to not be confined into the genre it’s paying homage to. Part thriller, part comedy, part serious character study, and featuring some seriously hyper-stylised shots, it’s incredible to think how fresh this film feels despite being made in the 1960s. 


### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Shoot the Piano Player is one of my favorites. I was thinking about it the other day and how the cutaway gag about his mother having a heart attack was effective and funny but Family Guy has since run that type of joke telling into the ground, similar to how Arrested Development and The Office ran the joke of someone saying something and the narrator or cutaway commenter revealing the opposite into the ground.

On a similar note, not to steer the conversation off topic, the Important Cinema Club podcast recently made a comment about how Shanghai Knights was a final lap for a lot of classic gags (like trying to disturb guards at Buckingham Palace) and I am now wondering if the modern dearth of comedy is due to so many jokes, and joke styles/archetypes, having been done to death/run out of juice.

## 4. What's Missing in Asghar Farhadi's The Salesman (2016)

**Score:** 3
**Body:** What a great movie. 

Much has already been written about the wife Rana's sexual assault, the shame both she and her husband Emad feel in the aftermath (which is socially regressive given their position among the intelligentsia), and their respective quests for some sort of justice, vengeance, or perhaps just forgetting. But just as in Farhadi's *A Separation* (2011, also incredible), here too interpersonal failure is a symptom of a broader social failure, in this case manifested in the otherwise absent (repressed) previous tenant. Sadly, less is generally written about her, outside of her direct relationship with the plot, so although I'm many years late, I wanted to spare a few words for her here.

In short, many if not all of the on-screen interpersonal conflicts are experienced ten-fold by the previous tenant, off screen. For example:

* Rana is ashamed of her sexual assault and the concomitant social stigma, but the previous tenant works as a prostitute and so literally cannot be shown on screen.
* Emad fears for the safety of a child that might be born in the apartment, forgetting that the previous tenant already had a child there.
* The neighbors speak of the violence they would inflict on Rana's attacker, but never of any action taken against the men who visited the previous tenant (e.g., Babak), much less any help they offered her.
* Rana is unhappy that the previous tenant left some of her belongings in the apartment, eventually dumping them outside to be destroyed by the rain. But the reason the previous tenant never picks up her stuff is because she is unable to find anywhere else to live.

Farhadi does a wonderful job highlighting the previous tenant's absence by instead substituting shots of her belongings abandoned in the courtyard, her child's drawings on the bedroom wall. More fundamentally, it is the previous tenant's absence that sets the conflict in motion, since it is her absence that allows Rana and Emad to move into the apartment in the first place.

Rana eventually prevails on Emad to let her attacker, the Old Man, leave without outing him in front of his family. The Old Man has a heart attack anyway, confirming the inescapability of Rana and Emad's own shame. There can be no happy ending for the characters on screen, then, while there is no happy ending for the characters off.

TL;DR - Rosebud was the name of the previous tenant.  


### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 5. Passing on Thoughts on Linklater's "Nouvelle Vague" and the Creator's High

**Score:** 3
**Body:** "Nouvelle Vague" is about the joy of being an artist. It’s about the runner’s high of creation, when you have a clear vision and the Tetris blocks are all falling into place. It’s about the daily spiritual fulfillment of doing the thing you love. I’m not an expert in the actual making of Breathless, but the film presents virtually no struggles; it’s just people having fun making something. And it’s all done in the very style that the characters themselves are creating: the French New Wave. Godard, Jean Seberg, and Jean-Paul Belmondo joke and play and flirt like they’re characters in "Jules and Jim." And if you’re looking for a modern director to capture the French New Wave’s mellow, improvisational style and reflective dialogue, there’s probably no better choice than the guy who made "Slacker."

As much breezy fun as I think the movie is, there was a moment about an hour in that Godard’s fortune cookie dialogue started to become grating. He’s very much presented as a card-carrying member of the first generation of film snobs, and since the film isn’t intending to create real three-dimensional versions of these real-life people, Godard in the film comes across as a pull-string doll. 

But realism is not the goal with "Nouvelle Vague." The Godard character, and the making of Breathless as a whole, is portrayed as what they mean to the filmmakers they inspired, rather than what they actually were. That’s not a knock against the movie–just a clarification of what it’s trying to be. This is a good-time Richard Linklater movie, not an in-depth biography. I do also think there’s something to be said about Godard’s pull-string dialogue being a good representation of the hydrology of influence. Water goes down your drain, it ends up in the sewer, then it goes to a treatment plant, is released into the river, which then takes it to the ocean where it’s evaporated, rained into a river, where it’s filtered and pumped into your house for you to wash dishes. Influence works much in the same way. 60 years after "Breathless," we now have an American filmmaker hugely influenced by a French filmmaker, making a film about that French filmmaker who is hugely influenced by American filmmakers. If you think of it that way, it almost makes Nouvelle Vague more special, as it becomes this closed loop moment–the water coming out of the faucet is now the water that went down the drain.

My full review can be found on YouTube right over here: [https://youtu.be/hXN1Ew7WSMc](https://youtu.be/hXN1Ew7WSMc)

1. I totally agree with you and think Nouvelle Vague is one of the more inspiring films about filmmaking and creativity in general. It is also fascinating to think that even today, there are still exchanges going back and forth between the French and Hollywood film industries. 

[I recently made a video essay on the production history of The 400 Blows that I think pairs nicely with Nouvelle Vague in grounding how artistically driven the French New Wave was. ](https://youtu.be/4uoTsBJ4SDE?si=zRrz9vM5u5TS4rk7)
2. Godard’s 24-aphorisms-a-second speaking style was probably toned down for Nouvelle Vague. He seemed to be much more pull-string dialogue in real life.

And having studied the making of Breathless, this movie is actually pretty accurate to what happened.
